---
title: 'R: Introduction and review'
subtitle: 'EC421, Set 01(R)'
author:
  - name: Ed Rubin
    email: edwardr@uoregon.edu
editor:
  render-on-save: true
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-level: 2
    theme: [../my.scss, default]
    highlight-style: gruvbox 
    embed-resources: true
    slide-number: c/t
    incremental: false
    controls-layout: edges
    transition: none
    preview-links: auto
    html-math-method: mathjax
    code-line-numbers: false
    auto-animate-unmatched: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE)
library(pacman)
p_load(leaflet, plotly, ggplot2, ggthemes, viridis, dplyr, magrittr, knitr)
red_pink = '#e64173'
dir_slides = '~/Dropbox/UO/Teaching/EC421W22/notes/01-intro/'
opts_chunk$set(
  # comment = '#>',
  fig.align = 'center',
  fig.height = 7,
  fig.width = 10.5,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE
)
```

## Econometrics

[Recall:]{.it .slab} Applied econometrics, data science, analytics require:

1. Intuition for the __theory__ behind statistics/econometrics<br>(assumptions, results, strengths, weaknesses).
2. Practical knowledge of how to __apply theoretical methods__ to data.
3. Efficient methods for __working with data__<br>(cleaning, aggregating, joining, visualizing).

__This course__ aims to deepen your knowledge in each of these three areas.

- 1: As before.
- 2-3: __R__

---

## {.center}

[R]{.bigger .slab}

---

## R

### What is R?

To quote the [R project website](https://www.r-project.org):

> R is a free software environment for statistical computing and graphics. It compiles and runs on a wide variety of UNIX platforms, Windows and MacOS.

::: {.fragment}
What does that mean?

- R was created for the statistical and graphical work required by econometrics.
- R has a vibrant, thriving online community. ([stack overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/r))
- Plus it's __free__ and __open source__.
:::

---

## R

### Why are we using R?

1. R is __free__ and __open source__ saving both you and the university 💰💵💰.
2. _Related:_ Outside of a small group of economists, private- and public-sector __employers favor R__ over [Stata]{.mono} and most competing softwares.
3. R is very __flexible and powerful__ adaptable to nearly any task, _e.g._, metrics, spatial data analysis, machine learning, web scraping, data cleaning, website building, teaching (these slides).

---

## R

### Why are we using R?

4. _Related:_ R imposes __no limitations__ on your amount of observations, variables, memory, or processing power. (I'm looking at __you__, [Stata]{.mono}.)
5. If you put in the work,<sup>†</sup> you will come away with a __valuable and marketable__ tool.
6. I 💖 __R__
7. R is a nice gateway to (and plays well with) other programming languages ([e.g.]{.it}, Python, SQL, C++, JavaScript).

::: {.footnote}
[†] Learning R definitely requires time and effort.
:::

---

```{r}
#| label: statistical-languages
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 11
#| dev: svg

pop_df = data.frame(
  lang = c('SQL', 'Python', 'R', 'SAS', 'Matlab', 'SPSS', 'Stata'),
  n_jobs = c(107130, 66976, 48772, 25644, 11464, 3717, 1624),
  free = c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
)
pop_df %<>% mutate(lang = lang %>% factor(ordered = TRUE))
ggplot(data = pop_df, aes(x = lang, y = n_jobs, fill = free)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  aes(x = reorder(lang, -n_jobs), fill = reorder(free, -free)) +
  labs(
    x = 'Statistical language',
    y = 'Number of jobs',
    title = 'Comparing statistical languages',
    subtitle = 'Number of job postings on Indeed.com, 2019/01/06'
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(label = scales::comma) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    'Free?',
    labels = c('True', 'False'),
    values = c(red_pink, 'darkslategray')
  ) +
  theme_pander(base_size = 22, base_family = 'Fira Sans Condensed') +
  theme(legend.position = 'bottom')
```

---

## {.center}

[R + Examples]{.bigger .slab}

---

## R + Data

```{r}
#| label: intro-gapminder
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: '1-2|3-4'

# Load the gapminder dataset (via the package)
library(gapminder)
# View the first 5 rows
gapminder |> head(5)
```

---

## R + Regression

```{r}
#| label: example-lm
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: '1-2|3-4'
#| output-location: fragment

# Fit a linear regression
fit = lm(lifeExp ~ 1 + I(gdpPercap / 1e3) + I(pop / 1e6), data = gapminder)
# Show the results
fit |> summary() |> coef()
```

---

## R + Regression

```{r}
#| label: example-lm-tidy
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: '3-4'

# Fit a linear regression
fit = lm(lifeExp ~ 1 + I(gdpPercap / 1e3) + I(pop / 1e6), data = gapminder)
# 'tidy' the results (from the broom package)
fit |> broom::tidy()
```

---

## R + Plotting (w/ [plot]{.mono})

```{r}
#| label: example-plot
#| echo: false
#| dev: svg

# Load packages with dataset
library(gapminder)

# Create simple plot
plot(
  x = gapminder$gdpPercap,
  y = gapminder$lifeExp,
  xlab = 'GDP per capita',
  ylab = 'Life Expectancy'
)
```

---

## R + Plotting (w/ [plot]{.mono})

```{r}
#| label: example-plot-code
#| eval: false
#| echo: true

# Load packages with dataset
library(gapminder)

# Create simple plot
plot(
  x = gapminder$gdpPercap,
  y = gapminder$lifeExp,
  xlab = 'GDP per capita',
  ylab = 'Life Expectancy'
)
```

---

## R + Plotting (w/ [ggplot2]{.mono})

```{r}
#| label: example-ggplot2-v1
#| echo: false
#| dev: svg

# Load packages
library(gapminder)
library(dplyr)

# Plot with ggplot2
ggplot(data = gapminder, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.75) +
  scale_x_continuous('GDP per capita', label = scales::comma) +
  labs(y = 'Life Expectancy') +
  theme_pander(base_size = 16)
```

---

## R + Plotting (w/ [ggplot2]{.mono})

```{r}
#| label: example-ggplot2-v1-code
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: '4-11'

# Load packages
library(gapminder)
library(dplyr)

# Plot with ggplot2
ggplot(data = gapminder, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.75) +
  scale_x_continuous('GDP per capita', label = scales::comma) +
  labs(y = 'Life Expectancy') +
  theme_pander(base_size = 16)
```

---

## R + More plotting (w/ [ggplot2]{.mono})

```{r}
#| label: example-ggplot2-v2
#| echo: false
#| dev: svg

library(gapminder)
library(dplyr)

ggplot(
  data = filter(gapminder, year %in% c(1952, 2002)),
  aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, color = continent, group = country)
) +
  geom_path(alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = as.character(year), size = pop), alpha = 0.75) +
  scale_x_log10('GDP per capita', label = scales::comma) +
  labs(y = 'Life Expectancy') +
  scale_shape_manual('Year', values = c(1, 17)) +
  scale_color_viridis('Continent', discrete = TRUE, end = 0.95) +
  guides(size = FALSE) +
  theme_pander(base_size = 16)
```

Notice we've moved to *log-10 scale* on the $x$-axis.

---

## R + More plotting (w/ [ggplot2]{.mono})

```{r}
#| label: example-ggplot2-v2-code
#| eval: false
#| echo: true

# Plot with ggplot2
ggplot(
  data = filter(gapminder, year %in% c(1952, 2002)),
  aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, color = continent, group = country)
) +
  geom_path(alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = as.character(year), size = pop), alpha = 0.75) +
  scale_x_log10('GDP per capita', label = scales::comma) +
  labs(y = 'Life Expectancy') +
  scale_shape_manual('Year', values = c(1, 17)) +
  scale_color_viridis('Continent', discrete = TRUE, end = 0.95) +
  guides(size = FALSE) +
  theme_pander(base_size = 16)
```

---

## R + Animated plots (w/ [gganimate]{.mono})

```{r}
#| label: example-gganimate
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| eval: false

library(gganimate)
gg = ggplot(
  data = gapminder %>% filter(continent != 'Oceania'),
  aes(gdpPercap, lifeExp, size = pop, color = country)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  scale_x_log10('GDP per capita', label = scales::comma) +
  facet_wrap(~continent) +
  theme_pander(base_size = 16) +
  theme(panel.border = element_rect(color = 'grey90', fill = NA)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}', y = 'Life Expectancy') +
  transition_time(year) +
  ease_aes('linear')
anim_save(
  animation = gg,
  filename = 'ex_gganimate.mp4',
  path = dir_slides,
  width = 10.5,
  height = 7,
  units = 'in',
  res = 150,
  nframes = 56
)
```

![Gapminder](ex_gganimate.gif)

---

## R + Animated plots (w/ [gganimate]{.mono})

```{r}
#| label: example-gganimate-code
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: '12-15'

library(gganimate)
ggplot(
  data = gapminder |> filter(continent != 'Oceania'),
  aes(gdpPercap, lifeExp, size = pop, color = country)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  scale_x_log10('GDP per capita', label = scales::comma) +
  facet_wrap(~continent) +
  theme_pander(base_size = 16) +
  theme(panel.border = element_rect(color = 'grey90', fill = NA)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}', y = 'Life Expectancy') +
  transition_time(year) +
  ease_aes('linear')
```

---

## R + Interactive plots (w/ [plotly]{.mono})

```{r}
#| label: example-plotly
#| echo: false

plot_ly(
  data = gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007),
  x = ~gdpPercap,
  y = ~lifeExp,
  type = 'scatter',
  mode = 'markers',
  size = ~pop,
  color = ~continent,
  colors = viridis::plasma(n = 5, end = .93),
  text = ~paste(
    'Country: ', country,
    '<br>GDP per capita:', scales::dollar(gdpPercap, 1),
    '<br>Life Expectancy:', scales::comma(lifeExp, 1),
    '<br>Population:', scales::comma(pop)
  ),
  hoverinfo = 'text',
  sizes = c(5, 100)
) |>
  layout(
    title = 'Gapminder data in 2007',
    xaxis = list(title = 'GDP per capita (log scale)', type = 'log'),
    yaxis = list(title = 'Life Expectancy')
  )
```

---

##

```{r}
#| label: example-plotly-code
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

plot_ly(
  data = gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007),
  x = ~gdpPercap,
  y = ~lifeExp,
  type = 'scatter',
  mode = 'markers',
  size = ~pop,
  color = ~continent,
  colors = viridis::plasma(n = 5, end = .93),
  text = ~paste(
    'Country: ', country,
    '<br>GDP per capita:', scales::dollar(gdpPercap, 1),
    '<br>Life Expectancy:', scales::comma(lifeExp, 1),
    '<br>Population:', scales::comma(pop)
  ),
  hoverinfo = 'text',
  sizes = c(5, 100)
) |>
  layout(
    title = 'Gapminder data in 2007',
    xaxis = list(title = 'GDP per capita (log scale)', type = 'log'),
    yaxis = list(title = 'Life Expectancy')
  )
```

---

## R + Maps

```{r}
#| label: example-leaflet
#| fig-height: 5
#| dev: svg

library(leaflet)
leaflet() %>%
  addTiles() %>%
  addMarkers(lng = -123.075, lat = 44.045, popup = 'The University of Oregon')
```

---

## {.center}

[Getting started with R]{.bigger .slab}

---

## Starting R

### Installation

- Install [R](https://www.r-project.org/).
- Install [RStudio](https://www.rstudio.com/products/rstudio/download/preview/){.mono}.
- Install [Quarto](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/){.mono}.
- __Optional/Overkill:__ [Git](https://git-scm.com/downloads){.mono}
  - Create an account on [GitHub](https://github.com/){.mono}
  - Register for a student/educator [discount](https://education.github.com/discount_requests/new).
  - For installation guidance and troubleshooting, check out Jenny Bryan's [website](http://happygitwithr.com/).
- __Note:__ Many UO labs have R installed and ready (helpful in a pinch).

---

## R resources

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width = '50%'}
#### Free(-ish)

- Time
- Google (inevitably StackOverflow)
- [Codex](https://openai.com/codex/), [Claude](https://claude.ai/), [Copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot), ...
- [Data services at the UO library](https://library.uoregon.edu/data-services)
- Your classmates
- Your GE and me
- R resources, *e.g.*, [here](http://edrub.in/ARE212/resources.html) and [here](https://education.rstudio.com/)
- [swirl](https://swirlstats.com/){.mono} and [learnr](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/learnr/index.html)
:::

::: {.column width = '50%'}
#### Money

Short online courses, *e.g.*, [DataCamp](https://www.datacamp.com)
:::
::::

---

## Starting R

### Some R basics

You will dive deeper into R in lab, but here are six big points about R:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width = '63%'}
1. Everything is an __object__.
2. Every object has a __name__ and __value__.
3. You use __functions__ on these objects.
4. Functions come in __libraries__ (__packages__)
5. R will try to __help__ you.
6. R has its __quirks__.
:::

::: {.column width = '31%'}
[foo]{.mono}

[foo = 2]{.mono}

[mean(foo)]{.mono}

[library(dplyr)]{.mono}

[?dplyr]{.mono}

[NA; error; warning]{.mono}
:::
::::

---

## Starting R {visibility='hidden'}

### R _vs._ [Stata]{.mono}

Coming from [Stata]{.mono}, here are a few important changes (benefits):

- Multiple objects and arrays (_e.g._, data frames) can exist in the same workspace (in memory). No more `keep`, `preserve`, `restore`, `snapshot` nonsense!
- (Base) R comes with lots of useful built-in functions and provides all the tools necessary for you to build your own functions. However, many of the _best_ functions come from external libraries.
- You don't need to `tset` or `xtset` data (you can if you really want... `ts`).

---

## {.center}

[Next:]{.it .slab} Metrics review(s)
